items.correct: Proteins denaturing and bonding to form a solid structure when heated.
items.misconception: The egg shell breaking when boiled.
items.why: Students think coagulation is something physical happening to the shell. It's PROTEIN CHEMISTRY: heat unfolds the proteins, which then bond together, turning liquid egg into solid - what makes a fried egg set.

items.q: Why are dried foods less prone to spoilage?
items.correct: Low water activity prevents microorganisms from growing.
items.misconception: Bacteria do not like dry textures.
items.why: Students think it's about texture preference. Microbes need available water. Drying reduces WATER ACTIVITY below the threshold microbes can use - no water = no metabolism = no spoilage.

items.q: Why must the 14 major allergens be highlighted on UK food labels?
items.correct: Reactions can be severe or life-threatening, so consumers must identify them at a glance.
items.misconception: To advertise that the food contains them.
items.why: Students miss the consumer-safety logic. Labelling laws require allergens to be visibly listed (bold/highlighted) so allergic people can quickly check. Penalties for non-compliance can be severe.

items.q: How many essential amino acids are there for adults?
items.why: It's 9. “12” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is a HBV protein source?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: soya. “wheat” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many kcal does 1 g of protein provide?
items.why: The real figure is 4 kcal/g. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
